behaviors.description: The cron parser rejects invalid expressions (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with a descriptive error message.
behaviors.justification: Input validation is pure algorithmic logic with no I/O, external dependencies, or non-determinism. The parser receives a string and validates it against known constraints. A malformed expression bug (e.g., accepting month 13) can be caught entirely with parameterized unit tests feeding various invalid inputs and verifying rejection with appropriate error messages. No system interaction, no mocking needed beyond standard assertion.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit test covering invalid cron expressions: out-of-range fields (month 13, day 32, hour 25), malformed syntax (extra/missing fields, invalid keywords), and boundary violations. Each test verifies rejection with an error message identifying the field and constraint violated.

behaviors.description: The next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like 'last day of month' and 'nearest weekday.'
behaviors.justification: Calendar and timestamp calculations are deterministic algorithmic logic. Given a fixed reference time and cron expression, the output is always the same—no system clock dependency, no external API. Edge cases (leap years, month lengths, last-day logic, weekday rounding) are all computable without I/O. A well-designed API accepts a reference time parameter or you mock system time in-process (still unit-level). Real failures (off-by-one month, wrong leap-year handling, incorrect last-day detection) can be caught entirely with parametrized unit tests using various reference times.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit tests with fixed reference times and diverse cron expressions: standard minute/hour/day patterns, edge cases (last-day-of-month for February in leap/non-leap years, nearest-weekday patterns), and boundary moments (midnight transitions, end-of-day). Verify computed next-run timestamps are correct and consistent with timezone representation.

behaviors.description: The overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes) and reports the conflicting time windows.
behaviors.justification: Overlap detection is pure range/interval logic: given two schedule objects with computed run times, determine if they overlap or collide within a gap threshold. No I/O, no external state, no non-determinism. Real failures (boundary condition bugs like off-by-one at exactly-the-gap-time, false positives, false negatives) can all be caught by unit tests comparing schedule pairs. No need to actually execute jobs or interact with the OS.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit tests with pairs of schedule objects at various temporal relationships: overlapping windows, exactly at minimum-gap boundary (critical boundary case), separated beyond gap, identical start times, one schedule nested inside another. Verify detector correctly identifies conflicts/non-conflicts and reports accurate conflicting time windows.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across a DST transition — a job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles the spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity correctly.
behaviors.justification: DST transitions are deterministic calendar rules, not non-deterministic external behavior. Testing correctness requires real timezone library objects (e.g., pytz, zoneinfo) rather than mocks, but does not require system-level interaction. The critical failures (treating spring-forward gap as a valid time, picking wrong occurrence in fall-back ambiguity, using naive datetime instead of timezone-aware) can be caught by unit tests using actual timezone definitions and verifying calculation logic around known DST transition moments. No OS environment changes or subprocess calls needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit tests with real timezone objects (not mocked) for a timezone with known DST transitions (e.g., US/Eastern with 2024-03-10 spring-forward and 2024-11-03 fall-back). Schedule a job at 2:30 AM and compute next-run times across transition boundaries: verify spring-forward gap is skipped (2:30 AM does not exist, next run jumps to 3:30 AM EDT), and fall-back ambiguity resolves consistently (correct occurrence selected).

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's computed next run time has already passed (e.g., system was down), the engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on the job's configured missed-run policy.
behaviors.justification: Missed-run policy application is pure decision logic: given a job configuration (policy flag), a schedule, and a reference time in the past, determine whether to skip or backfill missed runs. No job execution, no actual delays, no external state. Real failures (wrong policy field read, off-by-one in backfill count, incorrect skip logic) can be caught entirely with unit tests varying policy flags and past reference times. No I/O or integration needed.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized unit tests with job configurations covering both skip and backfill policies (skip_missed_runs=true/false, backfill_missed_runs=true/false), a reference time in the past relative to schedule start, and diverse schedule patterns (daily, weekly, hourly). Verify the scheduler applies the correct policy: skips the appropriate number of runs for skip policy, correctly computes backfill for backfill policy.
